stem: Find the surface area of the portion of the plane \( z = ([[alpha]])x + ([[beta]])y \) lying above the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \). (This is the scalar surface integral \( \iint_S 1\,dS \).)
solution: For \( z=g(x,y) \), \( dS=\sqrt{1+g_x^{2}+g_y^{2}}\,dA=\sqrt{1+([[alpha]])^{2}+([[beta]])^{2}}\,dA \). Since the integrand is constant, the area is \( \sqrt{1+([[alpha]])^{2}+([[beta]])^{2}}\cdot[[W]]\cdot[[H]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the flat base area \( [[W]]\cdot[[H]] \), ignoring the tilt of the plane.
This forgets the square root in \( dS \).
This adds the base area to the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct area.
This is off by one from the correct area.
This is off by two from the correct area.

stem: A thin sheet occupies the portion \( S \) of the plane \( z = ([[alpha]])x + ([[beta]])y \) lying above the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \). Evaluate the scalar surface integral \( \displaystyle\iint_S z\,dS \) (i.e., integrate the height function \( z \) over the surface \( S \), not just its area).
solution: Since \( z=g(x,y)=([[alpha]])x+([[beta]])y \), \( dS=\sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}\,dA=\sqrt{1+([[alpha]])^2+([[beta]])^2}\,dA \) (constant, since the surface is planar). So \( \iint_S z\,dS = \sqrt{1+([[alpha]])^2+([[beta]])^2}\displaystyle\int_0^{[[H]]}\!\!\int_0^{[[W]]} \big(([[alpha]])x+([[beta]])y\big)\,dx\,dy = \sqrt{1+([[alpha]])^2+([[beta]])^2}\left(\dfrac{([[alpha]])[[W]]^2[[H]]}{2}+\dfrac{([[beta]])[[H]]^2[[W]]}{2}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This integrates \( z \) over the flat rectangle \( dA \) instead of over the surface \( dS \), dropping the \( \sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2} \) factor.
This computes the plain surface AREA \( \iint_S 1\,dS \) instead of \( \iint_S z\,dS \).
This evaluates \( z \) only at the rectangle's midpoint and multiplies by the area, instead of integrating \( z \) over the region.
This swaps the roles of \( [[W]] \) and \( [[H]] \) in the two terms of the antiderivative.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The plane \( ([[p]])x + ([[q]])y + ([[r]])z = [[n]] \) cuts off a triangular region in the first octant (where \( x,y,z \ge 0 \)). Find the surface area of this triangular portion of the plane.
solution: Solving for \( z \): \( z=\dfrac{[[n]]-([[p]])x-([[q]])y}{[[r]]} \), so \( g_x=-\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[r]]}, g_y=-\dfrac{[[q]]}{[[r]]} \), and \( dS=\sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}\,dA=\dfrac{\sqrt{([[p]])^2+([[q]])^2+([[r]])^2}}{[[r]]}\,dA \) (constant, since the plane is flat). The plane meets the axes at \( x=[[n]]/[[p]] \) and \( y=[[n]]/[[q]] \), so its shadow in the \( xy \)-plane is a right triangle with legs \( [[n]]/[[p]] \) and \( [[n]]/[[q]] \) and area \( \tfrac12\cdot\frac{[[n]]}{[[p]]}\cdot\frac{[[n]]}{[[q]]} \). Multiplying by the constant \( dS \) factor gives the surface area \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the flat shadow (projected) triangle's area in the \( xy \)-plane, omitting the \( dS \) tilt factor entirely.
This divides the tilt factor by \( [[p]] \) instead of by \( [[r]] \) (the coefficient of \( z \)).
This omits \( ([[r]])^2 \) from inside the square root, as if the plane had no \( z \)-tilt contribution.
This uses \( [[n]]\cdot[[n]] \) for the base area instead of the correct \( \tfrac12\cdot\frac{[[n]]}{[[p]]}\cdot\frac{[[n]]}{[[q]]} \).
This is off by one from the correct area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: Find the surface area of the portion of the plane \( z = ([[alpha]])x + ([[beta]])y \) that lies directly above the disk \( x^{2}+y^{2} \le [[R]]^{2} \) in the \( xy \)-plane.
solution: As in any planar graph, \( dS=\sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}\,dA=\sqrt{1+([[alpha]])^2+([[beta]])^2}\,dA \) is constant. The base region is the disk \( x^2+y^2\le [[R]]^2 \), with area \( \pi([[R]])^2 \). So the surface area is \( \sqrt{1+([[alpha]])^2+([[beta]])^2}\cdot\pi([[R]])^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the flat disk's area \( \pi R^2 \), ignoring the tilt factor of the plane entirely.
This uses the diameter \( 2R \) in place of the radius \( R \) when computing the disk's area.
This uses \( \pi R \) (circumference-like) instead of \( \pi R^2 \) for the disk's area.
This uses the area of a \( 2R\times 2R \) bounding square instead of the disk's area \( \pi R^2 \).
This adds an extra factor of \( \pi \) to the correct answer.
This subtracts an extra factor of \( \pi \) from the correct answer.
